chunk_summary: This section focuses on anthropometry-based malnutrition classification and referral thresholds for infants and young children under 2 years, addressing clinical decision pathways for feeding difficulties and growth faltering using WHO and national guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the anthropometric criteria for classifying malnutrition in children under 2 years.
Learn referral thresholds for severe acute malnutrition (SAM) and moderate acute malnutrition (MAM).
Explore clinical decision pathways for managing feeding difficulties and growth faltering.
Familiarize with WHO and national guidelines for malnutrition management.
key_facts: Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is defined by weight-for-height Z-score < -3 or visible severe wasting.
Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) is classified by weight-for-height Z-score between -2 and -3.
WHO recommends immediate referral for children with SAM to specialized care facilities.
IMNCI guidelines emphasize early identification and management of malnutrition at community and facility levels.
Anthropometric tools like mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) < 11.5 cm are critical for SAM diagnosis.
WHO child growth standards provide benchmarks for assessing growth faltering in children under 2 years.
reference_quote: WHO child growth standards provide a reliable framework for assessing malnutrition and growth faltering in children under 2 years. (Source: https://www.who.int/tools/child-growth)
document_key_topics: Referral thresholds for SAM and MAM
Clinical decision pathways for feeding difficulties
Growth faltering in children under 2 years
